source_documents.title: Annex — Sovereignty Assessment Calculator (Cloud III DPS)
Cloud and AI Development Act — Proposed Regulation

seal_levels.name: No Sovereignty
seal_levels.description: Service, technology or operations under exclusive control of non-EU third parties, governed entirely in non-EU jurisdictions.
seal_levels.description_generalized: Service, technology or operations under exclusive control of extra-national third parties, governed entirely outside the assessed jurisdiction.

seal_levels.name: Jurisdictional Sovereignty
seal_levels.description: EU law formally applies with limited practical enforceability; service, technology or operations under exclusive control of non-EU third parties.
seal_levels.description_generalized: National law formally applies with limited practical enforceability; service, technology or operations under exclusive control of extra-national third parties.

seal_levels.name: Data Sovereignty
seal_levels.description: EU law applicable and enforceable, with material non-EU dependencies remaining; service, technology or operations under indirect control of non-EU third parties.
seal_levels.description_generalized: National law applicable and enforceable, with material extra-national dependencies remaining; service, technology or operations under indirect control of extra-national third parties.

seal_levels.name: Digital Resilience
seal_levels.description: EU law applicable and enforceable, EU actors exercising meaningful but not full influence; service, technology or operations under marginal control of non-EU third parties.
seal_levels.description_generalized: National law applicable and enforceable, national actors exercising meaningful but not full influence; service, technology or operations under marginal control of extra-national third parties.

seal_levels.name: Full Digital Sovereignty
seal_levels.description: Technology and operations under complete EU control, subject only to EU law, with no critical non-EU dependencies.
seal_levels.description_generalized: Technology and operations under complete national control, subject only to national law, with no critical extra-national dependencies.

objectives.title: Strategic Sovereignty
objectives.description: Strategic sovereignty captures the degree to which the services of a cloud provider are anchored within the European Union legal, financial, and industrial ecosystem. It assesses ownership stability, governance influence, and alignment with EU strategic priorities.

questions.title: Jurisdiction

bloc.text: Does the cloud service provider operate under {{BLOC}} jurisdiction, with contract governance and dispute resolution?

national.text: Does the cloud service provider operate under {{COUNTRY}} jurisdiction, with contract governance and dispute resolution?

questions.supplementary_info: If restrictions are imposed by cloud service customers for the chosen country, they should be justified, and it should be ensured that their implementation, for example in procurement procedures, is legally permissible on the basis of reasons such as public safety.

questions.title: Registered Office

bloc.text: Does the cloud service provider have a registered head office in {{BLOC}}?

national.text: Does the cloud service provider have a registered head office in {{COUNTRY}}?

questions.supplementary_info: L1(a) requires the provider to be established in the Union. From L2(a) onward, the requirement extends to all subcontractors involved in provision of the service. Annex III §1 tests genuine and stable presence: registered office, central administration, main establishment, EU banking/accounting relationships. Mere registration without operational substance does not satisfy L2+(a).
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-1 CF: 'Ensuring that bodies having decisive authority over your services are located within EU jurisdiction.' A registered head office is the primary legal indicator of this jurisdictional anchoring under EU company law. EU-CSF names the control objective; registered-office location is the standard evidentiary proxy.
questions.cada_annex_ref: Annex II L1(a) (provider); L2+(a) (extends to subcontractors involved in provision)

questions.title: CSP Effective Control

bloc.text: Is the cloud service provider effectively controlled by one or more {{BLOC}} corporations, with effective controls transparent to customers?

national.text: Is the cloud service provider under the effective control of one or more {{COUNTRY}} undertakings, with effective controls transparent to customers?

questions.supplementary_info: Effective control means the possibility to exert direct or indirect influence, or determine the key strategic, financial, or operational decisions from non-{{BLOC}} undertakings.

questions.title: CSP Control Change
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider commit to inform customers 90 days in advance of any actual changes affecting control that could undermine the sovereignty controls assessed in this tool (e.g., changes to ownership, shareholding, or governance)?
questions.supplementary_info: A 90-day advance notice gives customers time to assess and act before sovereignty is undermined. When assessing under C3A, “C3A controls” refers to the criteria in C3A §2 (SOV-1 through SOV-6).
questions.text_c3a: The cloud service provider MUST inform cloud service customers 90 days in advance of any actual changes affecting the cloud service provider’s control that could undermine or affect the C3A controls associated with the cloud service, including significant changes to ownership, shareholding, or governance relationships of the cloud service provider.

questions.title: EU Financing & Investment Anchoring
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider rely predominantly on EU-sourced financing (EU-domiciled institutional investors, EU public funding, EU capital markets), and can it demonstrate material investment in EU infrastructure, EU-based jobs, and EU value creation as part of its core operating model?
questions.text_generalized: Does the cloud service provider rely predominantly on domestically-sourced or regionally-sourced financing, and can it demonstrate material investment in local infrastructure, local jobs, and local value creation as part of its core operating model?
questions.supplementary_info: "EU-sourced financing" covers investors incorporated and tax-resident in EU member states, EIB/EIF instruments, and EU structural funds. Non-EU minority shareholders or bond-holders do not automatically disqualify, but a majority-non-EU investor base is material evidence against this criterion.

source.clause: §4 SOV-1 contributing factor: EU-sourced financing and EU investment

questions.title: EU Strategic Initiative Alignment
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider actively participate in EU-level digital sovereignty initiatives (e.g., Gaia-X, EUCS, IPCEI-CIS, EU Sovereign Cloud Label) and demonstrate consistency with the EU's digital, green, and industrial sovereignty objectives?
questions.text_generalized: Does the cloud service provider actively participate in nationally- or regionally-endorsed cloud sovereignty or digital independence initiatives, and demonstrate consistency with the country's stated digital strategy objectives?
questions.supplementary_info: Participation is evidenced by formal membership, published contributions, or certified compliance with initiative requirements — not self-declaration of alignment. Certification under EUCS (ENISA EU Cloud Services scheme) is the strongest evidence.

source.clause: §4 SOV-1 contributing factor: involvement in EU initiatives and digital sovereignty objectives

questions.title: Operational Resilience Against Coercion
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider have documented legal, operational, and technical measures enabling it to continue providing the contracted service even if subject to a foreign government request to cease or suspend operations, or if a key upstream vendor withdraws support?
questions.supplementary_info: Evidence includes: legal opinion on the provider's obligations under competing jurisdictions, vendor-independent operational runbooks covering the top-3 upstream dependencies, and at least one documented contingency drill in the past 12 months.

source.clause: §4 SOV-1 contributing factor: sustain operations against suspension requests or vendor disruption

questions.title: Roadmap Influence — EU Stakeholder Governance

bloc.text: Do EU stakeholders participate in formal governance bodies (e.g. advisory boards, customer councils, roadmap committees) that provide meaningful, documented influence over the cloud service provider's technology roadmap and service evolution?

source.clause: SOV-1 Q3: Control Over Roadmap — governance bodies with EU actor participation

national.text: Do EU actors exercise full and determinative influence over the provider's technology roadmap and service evolution, with binding governance rights that can override or direct provider decisions on matters affecting EU users?

source.clause: SOV-1 Q3: Control Over Roadmap — full EU actor influence

questions.supplementary_info: Evidence includes: governance charter or terms of reference for EU customer advisory council, documented roadmap arbitration outcomes, binding contractual rights to participate in product direction. A 'voice of customer' feedback portal alone does not meet the bloc threshold.

questions.title: Third-Country Subsidiary Legal Separation
questions.text: Where the cloud service provider has subsidiaries or affiliates established in third countries, does it maintain documented and enforced legal, technical, and organisational separation ensuring that EU operations, EU customer data, and EU service continuity cannot be directed, accessed, or disrupted by third-country parent, sibling, or affiliate entities?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Annex II L2(k) requires global providers to demonstrate effective separation of EU operations from 3rd-country subsidiaries. Evidence includes: governance separation policy, no privileged access from non-EU entities to EU production environments, redirect policy for foreign government requests.

questions.title: Associated Third-Country Commission Listing
questions.text: Is the third country (or country of the controlling legal entity) that exercises control over the cloud service provider included in the European Commission's published list of associated third countries adopted by implementing act for Union assurance level 3?
questions.supplementary_info: Only relevant where the provider is subject to third-country control. Art. 18(1) conditions for listing: GDPR adequacy decision; no measures conflicting with Data Act Art. 32(2)-(3) lawful-access requirements; no measures compelling service disruption or enforcement of third-country restrictive measures; no impediments to state-of-the-art service provision; open market to Union cloud services; reciprocal procurement access. Note: Annex II L3(g) cites 'Article 19' [sic — mechanism defined in Act Art. 18, 'Associated third countries'; see DR-F3].
questions.cada_annex_ref: Annex II L3(g) (derogation chapeau; cites 'Article 19' [sic — mechanism defined in Act Art. 18, 'Associated third countries']); Art. 18(3) (Commission publishes the list on its website)

questions.title: Material Licensing-Model Change Notice (LMIC)
questions.text: Where the provider (or its upstream platform vendor) makes a material change to the licensing model that would alter the cost structure, permitted use, or portability of the service, does the contract require: (a) timely advance notice to the customer; and (b) the customer’s right to terminate the contract without penalty before the change takes effect?

source.doc: SWIPO IaaS Code of Conduct v3.0
source.clause: TR05 (material portability changes — notice + termination right)

lmic_anchors.ref: §4 SOV-1 contributing factor 2 (‘assurances against change of control’)
§13.3 (notification of material changes to the outsourcing arrangement)

lmic_anchors.framework: SWIPO IaaS Code of Conduct v3.0
lmic_anchors.ref: TR05 (CSP shall inform CSC in a timely manner of changes that would materially alter portability, incl. costs; CSC should have the right to terminate in advance)
lmic_anchors.note: Requirement IDs verified against the Code structure as reproduced in published CSP transparency statements.

questions.lmic_rationale: The Broadcom/VMware licensing upheaval demonstrated that a licensing-model change at a stack vendor propagates to sovereign-cloud customers exactly like a change of control: costs escalate, portability degrades, and exit becomes expensive at the worst moment. EU-CSF CF SOV-1-2 already scores ‘assurances against change of control’; SWIPO TR05 provides verbatim industry-code language for the notice + termination right. This question extends both to cover licensing-model changes specifically.

questions.title: Local Investment, Employment and Knowledge Transfer (LMIC)
questions.text: Does the provider demonstrate investment, employment, and value creation in the customer’s country attributable to the service — including local hiring commitments, training and certification programs, and knowledge transfer to local staff or the customer’s personnel?

source.clause: §4 SOV-1 contributing factor 4 (‘Extent of investment, jobs, and value creation within EU’ — bloc substituted)

lmic_anchors.ref: §4 SOV-1 contributing factor 4 (‘Extent of investment, jobs, and value creation within EU’ — bloc substituted)

lmic_anchors.framework: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers, 7th ed. (Sep 2025)
lmic_anchors.ref: §5.54 (local labor participation requirement: minimum % of total labor cost to local staff; Rated Criteria for skills development); §§4.2-4.3 (PPSD must address the requirement)
lmic_anchors.note: §5.54 formally applies to works contracts; cited as the Bank-native pattern extended by analogy to cloud services, with EU-CSF §4 SOV-4 CF-3 as the primary criterion source.

questions.lmic_rationale: Verbatim EU-CSF contributing factor with the bloc substituted — the EU scores exactly this for itself — now reinforced by the World Bank’s own procurement framework (7th ed. §5.54) adding a local labor participation requirement. The Bank’s Regulations (7th ed., §5.54) impose a local-labor minimum and contemplate Rated Criteria for skills development in works procurement; this question applies the same Bank-endorsed mechanism to cloud services, where the primary source remains EU-CSF’s talent-pool contributing factor. §5.54 formally covers works contracts; cited here by analogy to cloud operations with that scope noted.

questions.title: Local Operating Staff Ratio (LMIC)
questions.text: What proportion of the personnel operating, supporting, and maintaining the service for this country’s customers are nationals or long-term residents of that country?
questions.tiers_note: ADOPTED-FROM: World Bank Procurement Regulations 7th ed. (Sep 2025) §5.54 (30% local labor participation floor; applies to works contracts; cited by analogy for cloud operations staffing). Bands 30-50/50-75/>75 are above-floor CSI progression (DR-L9). Where a project’s SPD/PPSD establishes a different percentage, that figure governs.

lmic_anchors.framework: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers, 7th ed. (Sep 2025)
lmic_anchors.ref: §5.54 (local labor participation minimum)
lmic_anchors.note: Applied by analogy from works contracts to cloud operations staffing — see DR-L6.

lmic_anchors.ref: §4 SOV-4 contributing factor 3 (‘EU-based talent pool with expertise to operate and sustain the service’ — jurisdiction substituted)

questions.lmic_rationale: Four progressive bands above/below the World Bank 30% floor instead of a binary yes/no. The 30% boundary is adopted from WB PR 7th ed. §5.54; bands 30-50/50-75/>75 are CSI editorial progression. The 30% floor behavior of autonomyRungsUnlocked is unchanged — band b30_50 maps to staffPct=30 and satisfies the floor. DR-L9.

objectives.title: Legal & Jurisdictional Sovereignty
objectives.description: Legal & Jurisdictional sovereignty evaluates the legal environment, exposure to foreign authority, and enforceability of rights that govern the services of a technology provider. It determines the extent to which the services are anchored in European jurisdiction and insulated from external legal claims.

questions.title: Extraterritorial Exposure
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider, at least once a year, identify any laws from jurisdictions other than those in which the provider operates or the customer is located, that could compel the provider to disclose data, restrict access, suspend service, or otherwise act against the customer's interests — AND conduct a structured risk assessment of those laws?

questions.title: Audit Rights

bloc.text: Does the cloud service provider document procedures that allow the relevant federal or national cybersecurity authority (in the country where the data center is located) to verify compliance with sovereignty criteria by audit?
bloc.text_c3a: The cloud service provider MUST document procedures that allow the relevant federal or national cybersecurity authority to verify compliance with the C3A criteria by an audit. The responsible authority is the one in the country where the data center is located.

national.text: Does the cloud service provider document procedures that allow {{NATIONAL_ADMIN}} to verify compliance with sovereignty criteria by audit?
national.text_c3a: The cloud service provider MUST document procedures that allow {{NATIONAL_ADMIN}} to verify compliance with the C3A criteria by an audit.

questions.supplementary_info: Audit rights may be derived from contract or law. Authorities try to rely on existing audits (e.g., BSI C5, SOC 2 Type 2) before conducting their own. Fees shall not be so high as to effectively deny this right.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-2 CF: 'Existence of legal, contractual, or technical channels through which non-EU authorities could compel access to data or systems.' Audit rights operationalize the customer's ability to verify what access channels exist — the contractual enforcement mechanism for this CF. The EU-CSF framework does not name audit rights explicitly in SOV-2; the closest named CF is in SOV-7.
questions.cada_fidelity_rationale: CADA Annex III §7 mandates detailed ownership/control evidence that operationalizes audit rights for sovereignty assessment.

questions.title: State of Defense Takeover

bloc.text: If a {{BLOC}} member state declares a state of defense, can the cloud service provider enable that state to take over the capabilities required to operate the cloud (including physical assets and personnel) within the framework of legal possibilities?

national.text: If {{COUNTRY}} declares a state of defense, can the cloud service provider enable {{NATIONAL_ADMIN}} to take over the capabilities required to operate the cloud (including material assets and personnel) within the framework of legal possibilities?

questions.supplementary_info: This usually means that documentation, source code, and administration tools are held in a portable format so that the competent national authority can use them.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-2 CF: 'Applicability of international regimes, which may restrict usage or transfer' and SOV-1 CF: 'Ability to sustain secure operations against requests to cease or suspend the service.' The state-of-defense takeover question operationalizes the most extreme legal-regime case. EU-CSF names the principle; C3A defines the specific constitutional trigger (state of defense).

questions.title: Emergency Takeover — National Constitutional Regime
questions.text: If {{COUNTRY}} declares a constitutional state of emergency under {{EMERGENCY_REGIME}}, can the cloud service provider enable the competent national authority to take over the capabilities required to operate the cloud (including material assets, source code, administration tools, and personnel) within the framework of legal possibilities?

source.clause: SOV-2 / takeover readiness under emergency regime

questions.supplementary_info: This is the CSI-Composite-Generalized equivalent of C3A SOV-2-03 ("state of defense"), which is bound to specific EU constitutional concepts. Many LMIC constitutions provide for declared states of emergency, exception, or public calamity rather than "state of defense." This question generalizes the underlying control objective — sovereign takeover readiness — to those analogous regimes. Activated only in non-EU (Generalized) variant.

questions.title: IP Jurisdiction
questions.text: Is the intellectual property underlying the cloud service created, registered, and primarily developed within the EU, subject to EU intellectual property law, with no encumbering foreign IP claims that could restrict the customer's access to or use of the service under EU law?
questions.text_generalized: Is the intellectual property underlying the cloud service created, registered, and primarily developed within {{COUNTRY}} or {{BLOC}}, subject to national intellectual property law, with no encumbering foreign IP claims that could restrict the customer's access to or use of the service under national law?
questions.supplementary_info: IP domicile is assessed at the level of the core platform IP (not third-party libraries). Foreign subsidiary IP held by an EU parent is acceptable if it remains subject to EU law via contractual and corporate structure. Foreign IP held by a non-EU parent and licensed back creates material risk of access restriction.

source.clause: §4 SOV-2 contributing factor: location of IP creation, registration, and development

questions.title: Data Access Pathways — Non-EU Authority Compelled Access

bloc.text: Does the cloud service provider have implemented and demonstrated legal, technical, and organisational measures that prevent non-EU authorities from compelling access to customer data or systems through any channel — with all such requests consistently rejected and customers notified of any attempt?

source.clause: SOV-2 Q3: Data Access Pathways — consistent rejection of non-EU authority access requests

questions.supplementary_info: This question covers whether non-EU authorities (e.g. under FISA, CLOUD Act, Chinese Cybersecurity Law) have any enforceable channel to compel access to EU customer data or systems. Providers subject to such laws that have merely mitigation clauses (e.g. 'we will notify customers') do not pass this criterion. Only providers operating entirely outside the reach of such laws — or with legally verified structural immunity — pass. See XLSX SOV-2 Q3 and CADA L2(g)(iv).

questions.title: Third-Country Vulnerability Disclosure Prohibition
questions.text: Does the provider demonstrate — through legal analysis or independent verification — that no law or regulation in any third country applicable to the provider or its subcontractors requires disclosure of software vulnerabilities to that country's authorities before those vulnerabilities have been publicly remediated or disclosed?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Annex II L1(g) explicitly prohibits recognition at UAL 1 where such laws exist without demonstrated countermeasures. Evidence: legal opinion covering applicable jurisdictions, supplier self-declarations, independent legal review.

questions.title: Annual Independent Audit Renewal
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider commit to obtaining an annual independent audit opinion confirming continued compliance with the claimed Union Assurance Level, from an auditing organisation that meets the independence and competence requirements of CADA Article 20?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Art. 20(8) requires audited providers at UAL 2-4 to submit annual reviews. This is a procedural commitment question — evidence is a contractual clause or formal policy statement committing to annual renewal.

questions.title: Immunity from Third-Country Restrictive Measures
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider guarantee that no third country can force it to implement, enforce, give effect to, or comply with any restrictive measures such as sanction regimes, embargoes, or equivalent legal or administrative measures adopted by a third country, against the public sector body or the cloud service itself — unless the measure is legitimate under Member State or Union law?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Annex II L2(g)(iv) text verbatim: 'any legal or administrative measures by a third country requiring the cloud computing service provider to implement, enforce, give effect to, or comply with restrictive measures, such as sanction regimes, embargoes, or any equivalent legal or administrative measures adopted by a third country, will not prevent or impede [the provider] from providing the agreed cloud computing service, except where legitimate under Member State or Union law.' Evidence: contractual clause asserting this guarantee, legal opinion for providers subject to potential third-country export-control law, documented mechanism for contesting conflicting third-country orders.
questions.cada_annex_ref: Annex II L2(g)(iv); L3(g)(iv) (applies only under the Art. 18 associated-third-country derogation)
questions.cada_applicability_note: Binds only providers under third-country control (chapeau of L2(g)/L3(g)). At UAL 4 third-country control is prohibited outright (L4(g)), so this question is moot. Pending applicability_condition support, the question is shown at UAL 2–3 and excluded from the UAL 4 gate (DR-F5).

questions.title: Local Establishment and Contractual Enforceability (LMIC)
questions.text: Does the provider maintain a genuine and stable establishment in the customer’s country — or contract through a locally registered entity — against which the contract is enforceable in local courts or an agreed neutral arbitral forum, evidenced by local registration, physical presence or authorized representation, and locally executed contracting?

lmic_anchors.ref: Annex III §1 (genuine-and-stable establishment indicia: registration, premises, staff, contractual operations, banking) — ported from EU jurisdiction to customer jurisdiction
§12.2 (due diligence on the contracting counterparty)

questions.lmic_rationale: The indicia of ‘genuine and stable establishment’ are CADA’s own audit-evidence catalogue (Annex III §1), ported with one substitution: the jurisdiction. Enforceability is the LMIC analog of EU establishment — a sovereignty assessment that ignores whether the contract can actually be enforced locally measures paper rights.

objectives.title: Data & AI Sovereignty
objectives.description: Data sovereignty captures the degree to which cloud service customers retain control over where their data is stored, processed, and accessed, and whether they can independently manage encryption, identity, logging, and data flows without being dependent on the cloud service provider.

questions.title: Data Residence — Customer Data

bloc.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide a service option where cloud service customer data is exclusively stored and processed in {{BLOC}}.

national.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide a service option where cloud service customer data is exclusively stored and processed in {{COUNTRY}}.

questions.supplementary_info: Data residence options must be contractually guaranteed and verifiable by the customer.

questions.title: Data Residence — Transparency
questions.text: A cloud service customer MUST be able to check where their cloud service derived data, cloud service customer data, and account data are stored and processed.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-3 CF: 'Strict confinement of storage and processing to European jurisdictions, with no fallback to third countries.' Disclosure of data locations is a prerequisite for verifying confinement — without transparency, the customer cannot confirm this CF is met. Transparency is implied by the confinement standard but not named as a separate CF.
questions.cada_fidelity_rationale: CADA Annex III §3 requires detailed data flow diagrams demonstrating exclusive EU data storage and processing.

questions.title: Data Residence — Derived & Account Data in EU
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide a service option where cloud service derived data and account data are exclusively stored and processed in {{BLOC}}.

questions.title: Data Residence — Provider Data
questions.eu_csf_source_factor: SOV-3 / Strict confinement of storage and processing to European jurisdictions
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide a service option where cloud service provider data is exclusively stored and processed in {{BLOC}}.
questions.supplementary_info: Cloud service provider data covers configuration and utilization information of system components, storage and network resource allocations, physical and virtual resource failure rates, operational costs, etc. (C3A §1.5). It is under the control of the cloud service provider, distinct from customer data and derived data. Often overlooked because it is operational metadata, but its location can be a sovereignty exposure.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-3 CF: 'Strict confinement of storage and processing to European jurisdictions.' Provider-operational data (configuration, telemetry, resource allocations) is not named explicitly in the CF, but is subject to the same confinement principle. Provider data can contain sovereignty-sensitive operational intelligence about infrastructure and capacity.

questions.title: External Key Management
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST allow the integration of external encryption key management system for creating, managing, and storing encryption keys outside of the cloud service provider environment for the use of IaaS and PaaS, or provide functionally equivalent mechanisms that ensure the customer can only create, manage and store the encryption keys only outside of the cloud service provider environment.

questions.title: External Key Management — SaaS
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST allow the integration of external key management systems for creating, managing and storing keys outside of the cloud environment also for SaaS, or provide functionally equivalent mechanisms that ensure the customer can only create, manage and store the encryption keys outside of the cloud service provider environment.
questions.supplementary_info: The integration of external key management systems for IaaS and PaaS is widely implemented and commonly standardized. For SaaS solutions, external encryption key management systems are less common; therefore, cloud service providers should support external encryption key management capabilities for SaaS where technically feasible and appropriate to the service architecture. If this criterion is only fulfilled for some SaaS, the cloud service provider MUST provide a list of these services to the cloud services customer.

questions.title: External Identity Provider
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST support standards-based integration of external identity providers for authentication and access management for the cloud service.

questions.title: External Identity Provider — Open Standards
questions.text: The integration of an external Identity Provider MUST be implemented via open, non-proprietary standards.

questions.title: External Identity Provider — Stateless Authentication
questions.text: The provider MUST support a stateless authentication model that does not mandate the creation and copies of accounts within the provider's directory.

questions.title: External Identity Provider — Dynamic Claims
questions.text: Authorization MUST be controllable via dynamic claims and attributes issued directly by the customer's external identity provider.

questions.title: Logging and Monitoring
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide customers with the capability to record, retain, and review logs of management and data access activities related to cloud service customer data. These logs MUST enable customers to identify when access occurred, the identity associated with the request, and the relevant operational context available through the service's logging capabilities.

questions.title: Logging — Real-Time Open API
questions.text: The logging service MUST ensure full data flow transparency by providing real-time access via standardized open-source APIs.

questions.title: Logging — Granular Filtering
questions.text: The service MUST support granular filtering.

questions.title: Client-Side Encryption
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST enable client-side encryption of cloud service customer data. Whenever the cloud service customer data is transmitted, processed or stored inside the cloud environment, it MUST be encrypted with a private key that is only available to the cloud service customer outside of the cloud service provider environment.

questions.title: AI — Training Data Jurisdiction
questions.eu_csf_source_factor: SOV-3 / AI models and data pipelines developed, trained, hosted under EU control
questions.text: For any AI or ML model used to deliver the cloud service, the cloud service provider MUST disclose the jurisdictions in which the model's training data was collected, stored, and processed. Where training data is sourced from {{BLOC}}, the provider MUST be able to demonstrate it.
questions.supplementary_info: EU CSF v1.2.1 SOV-3 explicitly includes "extent to which AI models and data pipelines are developed, trained, hosted, and governed under EU control". Training data jurisdiction is the upstream determinant of model behaviour and legal exposure. Applies to all generative AI features, ML-based recommendations, and embedded model inference within the service.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-3 CF: 'AI models and data pipelines developed, trained, hosted, and governed under EU control.' Training data jurisdiction disclosure is the prerequisite for verifying EU-controlled training — you cannot substantiate that training occurred under EU control without disclosing where training data was collected and processed. EU-CSF names the control objective; the specific disclosure requirement is a CSI operationalization.
questions.cada_fidelity_rationale: L2(f) prohibits use of service-generated data for third-country AI training and prohibits any transfer outside the Union. This question asks only for training-data jurisdiction disclosure, which is weaker than the flat prohibition. It is inferred evidence under Annex III §6 (model cards / training-source records), not a direct mapping to L2(f).

questions.title: AI — Inference Location
questions.text: For any AI or ML model used to deliver the cloud service, the cloud service provider MUST ensure that inference (the processing of customer data through the model) occurs exclusively within {{BLOC}}. The provider MUST contractually guarantee no cross-border inference fallback.
questions.supplementary_info: Where customer data hits the model matters distinctly from where the model was trained. Inference can be the moment of exposure for sensitive prompts, customer documents, or PII. EU CSF SOV-3 covers this under "where data is processed". This is the AI-specific complement to SOV-3-01 data residency.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-3 CF: 'AI models and data pipelines hosted and governed under EU control.' Inference is the moment customer data is actively processed through the model — the 'hosted/processed' dimension of the CF. EU-CSF names the principle; this question requires contractual and technical enforcement of in-EU inference location.
questions.cada_fidelity_rationale: L2(f) prohibits use of service-generated data for third-country AI training and transfer outside the Union. This question addresses inference location (processing of customer data), which maps more naturally to L2(c)'s processing-localisation requirement. Re-tagged inferred from L2(c) rather than a direct mapping to L2(f).

questions.title: AI — Right to Opt Out of Training
questions.eu_csf_source_factor: SOV-3 / customer retains autonomy over AI capabilities
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST offer a contractual and technical opt-out from any use of customer data (cloud service customer data, cloud service derived data, account data, or prompts) for training, fine-tuning, or evaluating AI/ML models. Opt-out MUST be the default for sovereign tier customers.

source.clause: SOV-3 / customer retains autonomy over AI capabilities

questions.supplementary_info: This is the LMIC-relevant analogue of the EU AI Act's training-data provisions. In sovereign procurement, the risk is that customer data — even derived telemetry — becomes part of a global model, exporting national knowledge into a foreign-jurisdiction artefact.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-3 CF: 'AI models governed under EU control, minimizing dependency on non-EU technology stacks.' Customer opt-out from training data use is a governance control ensuring the customer retains sovereignty over how their data shapes model behaviour — the CF implies governance control but does not specify this mechanism.

questions.title: Prohibition on Third-Country AI Training Use and Data Transfer
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider guarantee that data generated by using the audited service will not be used to train or fine-tune any AI system operated by a third country or a legal entity established in a third country, and will not be transferred outside the Union in any case?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Annex II L2(f)/L3(f)/L4(f) verbatim: 'the data generated by using the audited service are not used to train or fine-tune any AI system operated by a third country or a legal entity established in a third-country, and are not transferred outside the Union in any case.' This is a flat prohibition — not an opt-out mechanism. Evidence per Annex III §6: contractual clauses prohibiting this use, data-flow diagrams covering all MLOps touchpoints, EU build/test/release records, model cards, data lineage documentation, and subcontractor list confirming no third-country ML processing.

questions.title: AI — Model Provider Control Chain
questions.eu_csf_source_factor: SOV-3 + SOV-5 / dependency on non-EU technology stacks
questions.text: For each AI or ML model integrated into the cloud service, the cloud service provider MUST disclose the model provider, its jurisdiction of incorporation, its effective control (parent, controlling shareholders, ultimate beneficial owners), and the legal regime governing its model weights and update pipeline.

source.clause: SOV-3 + SOV-5 / dependency on non-EU technology stacks

questions.supplementary_info: Extends C3A SOV-5 (supply chain) into the AI layer. The model provider is a critical upstream dependency that can change behaviour, licensing, or availability through unilateral action. SOV-1-style effective-control analysis applied to model providers.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-3 CF: 'Minimizing dependency on non-EU technology stacks.' Knowing the model provider's jurisdiction, ownership, and control chain is the prerequisite for assessing whether a dependency constitutes a non-EU technology stack risk. EU-CSF names the risk category; this question operationalizes the supply-chain disclosure needed to assess it.

questions.title: Verifiable Data Erasure
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider implement technical mechanisms guaranteeing irreversible removal of customer data — including customer data, derived data, and account data — upon request or contract termination, and can it provide verifiable evidence of erasure (e.g., cryptographic proof, audited deletion logs, or third-party attestation)?
questions.supplementary_info: Irreversible removal means data cannot be recovered by the CSP after erasure — not merely logical deletion from production systems. Evidence must satisfy the customer that data no longer exists anywhere in the CSP's infrastructure, including backups, logs, caches, and derived analytics. GDPR Article 17 establishes the right to erasure; this criterion operationalizes the technical capability behind that right.

source.clause: §4 SOV-3 contributing factor: mechanisms guaranteeing irreversible removal of data with verifiable evidence

questions.title: Sensitive Data Risk Assessment & Residency (UAL 4)
questions.text: Has the cloud service provider conducted a documented risk assessment that formally identifies which categories of customer data, metadata, and telemetry are classified as sensitive, and does it guarantee — without exceptions — that all such sensitive data is processed, stored, and transferred exclusively within the Union, including backups, replicas, and derived data?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA L4(c) requires a formal sensitivity classification process as a prerequisite: the residency obligation applies specifically to data 'identified as sensitive' following a risk assessment. This differs from L1(c)/L2(c) which apply universally to all customer data. Evidence: risk assessment documentation, data classification policy, technical controls preventing any non-EU transfer of classified-sensitive data.

objectives.title: Operational Sovereignty
objectives.description: Operational sovereignty assesses whether the cloud service provider maintains independent operational capability within the relevant jurisdiction, including personnel, access paths, connectivity, security operations, and the ability to operate autonomously in the event of external disconnection.

bloc.text: All personnel who have logical or physical access to infrastructure used to operate the cloud service, as well as those who are responsible for customer support, and all persons who have management control of the cloud service provider MUST be {{BLOC}} citizens with {{BLOC}} as main residency.

national.text: All personnel who have logical or physical access to infrastructure used to operate the cloud service, as well as those who are responsible for customer support, and all persons who have management control of the cloud service provider MUST be {{BLOC}} citizens with {{COUNTRY}} as main residency.

questions.title: Operating Personnel — Standalone European Organisation
questions.text: The operating personnel is part of an organization that MUST be a standalone European organization.

questions.title: Operating Personnel — Security-Cleared Local Residents
questions.text: Does the provider ensure that all personnel with privileged access to the service are security-cleared residents of {{COUNTRY}}, bound by confidentiality obligations under applicable national law?
questions.supplementary_info: This fallback question applies when the parent criterion (SOV-4-01) cannot be met. It provides partial credit for providers that maintain equivalent controls through national security clearance and residency requirements binding operating personnel to confidentiality under local law.

bloc.text: The cloud service provider MUST implement organizational and technical measures ensuring that administrative access to systems used to operate the cloud service is performed through access paths located within {{BLOC}}. Administrative access originating from locations outside {{BLOC}} MUST be technically restricted, except in narrowly defined and controlled exceptional scenarios that are subject to additional authorization and monitoring controls.

national.text: The cloud service provider MUST implement organizational and technical measures ensuring that administrative access to systems used to operate the cloud service is performed through access paths located within {{COUNTRY}}. Administrative access originating from locations outside {{COUNTRY}} MUST be technically restricted, except in narrowly defined and controlled exceptional scenarios that are subject to additional authorization and monitoring controls.

questions.title: Redundant Connectivity
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST ensure redundant and independent connectivity for the delivery of the sovereign cloud service. In the event of a disruption of one connectivity provider, alternative connectivity providers MUST be able to maintain connectivity in accordance with the contractual service level agreements. At least one of the connectivity providers MUST be an {{BLOC}} based company.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-4 CF: 'Capacity for EU operators to manage, maintain, and support the technology without requiring non-EU vendor involvement.' Redundant independent connectivity is the network infrastructure prerequisite for continuous EU-controlled operations — single-provider failure would collapse operational sovereignty regardless of personnel or process controls. Not named as a contributing factor in EU-CSF SOV-4.

questions.title: Redundant Connectivity — Independent of CSP Corporate Structure
questions.text: At least one of the connectivity providers is not part of the corporate structure of the cloud service provider.

questions.title: Redundant Connectivity — Multi-Homed BGP Fallback
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider implement multi-homed BGP routing across at least two distinct upstream connectivity paths, with documented failover behaviour and at least one annual failover test? Multi-homing MUST be implemented at the routing layer (not merely at the physical link layer) so that loss of a single upstream provider does not interrupt customer-facing service.

source.clause: SOV-4 / resilient connectivity under market constraints

questions.supplementary_info: Fallback for SOV-4-03 in markets where multiple commercially-independent national tier-1 ISPs do not exist. Multi-homing at the BGP layer is the underlying control objective the C3A criterion is reaching for — this fallback rewards providers that achieve it through architectural means even where the carrier market is concentrated. Activated only when the parent SOV-4-03 is answered No.

questions.title: Security Operations Centre

bloc.text: The cloud service provider MUST ensure that Security Operations Center (SOC) capabilities for the offered cloud services are established and operated within {{BLOC}}. In the case of a disconnect (SOV-4-10), a stand-alone and equivalent SOC MUST be provided in {{BLOC}}.

national.text: The cloud service provider MUST ensure that Security Operations Center (SOC) capabilities for the offered cloud services are established and operated within {{COUNTRY}}. In the case of a disconnect (SOV-4-10), a stand-alone and equivalent SOC MUST be provided in {{COUNTRY}}.

questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: EU-CSF explicitly places SOC under SOV-7 CF: 'Security Operations Centres and response teams operating exclusively under EU jurisdiction.' This criterion follows C3A §2.4.4 in classifying SOC as an operational sovereignty matter (SOV-4). When used in EU-CSF scoring, the question contributes to SOV-4 but its primary source reference is the SOV-7 contributing factor.

questions.title: Ingress Data Control
questions.text: All software updates and operational data affecting the cloud service MUST be received, authorized and validated in a secured network area managed and controlled by the cloud service provider. The cloud service provider MUST verify and check updates for known vulnerabilities. Updates MUST include documentation satisfying the needs of the cloud service provider. The update process MUST be based on a controlled change management processes.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-4 CF: 'Capacity for EU operators to manage without requiring non-EU vendor involvement.' Ingress data control gateways are the network-layer enforcement mechanism for EU-bounded operations — without controlled ingress, external actors can bypass EU operational controls.

questions.title: Ingress Data Control — Dedicated Physical Devices
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST implement the secure network area (e.g. DMZ) on dedicated physical devices.

questions.title: Ingress Data Control — Technical Documentation to Authority
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide technical documentation how the criterion SOV-4-05-C is implemented to the responsible cybersecurity authority if requested, in accordance with applicable law and established supervisory, cooperation agreements or audit mechanisms. The responsible authority is the one in the country where the data center is located. Such information may be provided through appropriate confidentiality protections and secure disclosure procedures.
questions.supplementary_info: A vulnerability is regarded as known, when it is listed in the European Union Vulnerability Database (EUVD) or in the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) Program from the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST).

questions.title: Update Threat Analysis
questions.text: When using third-party software under the cloud service provider's responsibility, the cloud service provider MUST implement risk-based security analysis prior to deployment, including measures to detect and mitigate malicious code, viruses, spyware, and ransomware.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-4 CF: 'Availability of full technical documentation, source code, and operational know-how enabling long-term autonomy.' Threat-aware update analysis is the operational practice through which this autonomy is maintained against supply-chain attacks on the software update pipeline.
questions.cada_fidelity_rationale: CADA L2(i)(ii) mandates controls to block remote tampering features and source code audits for 3rd-country software components.

questions.title: Data Exchange Monitoring
questions.text: Any cloud service derived data, cloud service customer data and account data exchanged between the cloud service provider and third parties MUST always be monitored, controlled and logged. In order to do so, the cloud service provider MUST establish a documented process. The documentation MUST be reviewed and updated regularly, at least once a year. The cloud service provider MUST document what kind of data is exchanged with third parties. This documentation MUST ensure that it is clear which data is flowing to which party and this can also be meaningfully aggregated. The cloud service provider MUST make this documentation available to the cloud service customer. It is acceptable that this is only made available to the customer if they have agreed to keep the information confidential and not publicly disclose it. The cloud service provider MUST clearly define the exchange format and document it as part of the data exchange documentation.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-4 CF: 'Assurance that operational support is delivered from within the EU.' Data exchange monitoring provides runtime verification that EU-bounded operations remain bounded — without monitoring, EU operational sovereignty is an unverifiable claim.

questions.title: Data Exchange Gateways
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST document, define, and visualize (via a Data Flow Diagram) all data exchanges between the cloud service provider and third parties of cloud service derived data, cloud service customer data, and account data. The data exchanges MUST occur only via known gateways. The documentation MUST clearly identify data origins, destinations, transport protocols, data type and security mechanisms protecting these exchanges. The documentation MUST be reviewed and updated regularly, at least once a year. This documentation does not need to be published publicly.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-4 CF: 'Capacity for EU operators to manage without requiring non-EU vendor involvement.' Data exchange gateways are the physical control points ensuring cross-boundary data flows remain under EU authority.

questions.title: Data Exchange Gateways — Data Flow Diagram to Authority
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide the Data Flow Diagram to the responsible cybersecurity authority if requested, in accordance with applicable law and established supervisory, cooperation agreements or audit mechanisms. The responsible authority is the one in the country where the data center is located. Such information may be provided through appropriate confidentiality protections and secure disclosure procedures.

questions.title: Disconnect
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST be able to disconnect all non-{{BLOC}} network-connections to the cloud without an impairment of the availability, integrity, authenticity and confidentiality of the cloud service. This includes all incoming updates and data exchanges with non-{{BLOC}} entities (including but not limited to: external heartbeat signals and global license validation servers) that are in the responsibility of the cloud service provider. The cloud service provider MUST establish and document a process, when and how a disconnect is executed. This process MUST be independent from non-{{BLOC}} entities. The cloud service provider MUST update this documentation regularly, at least once a year. The cloud service provider MUST conduct disconnection tests for ensuring the availability of all cloud services in case of a disconnection from the non-{{BLOC}} network-connections at least once a year. The cloud service provider MUST document these tests as part of the aforementioned documentations. The documentation MUST include, but is not limited to, the results of the performed test.

questions.title: Disconnect — Documentation to Authority
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST provide the documentation of the disconnect process and disconnection tests to the responsible cybersecurity authority if requested, in accordance with applicable law and established supervisory, cooperation agreements or audit mechanisms. Where relevant, the cloud service provider may provide supporting documentation. The responsible authority is the one in the country where the data center is located. Such information may be provided through appropriate confidentiality protections and secure disclosure procedures.
questions.supplementary_info: In the context of the disconnect requirement, network connections between the cloud service provider and cloud service customers are excluded from the scope of the disconnection capability.

questions.title: Disconnect / Reconnect — Documented Plan & Annual Exercise
questions.text: Does the provider maintain a documented disconnect and reconnect procedure, with evidence of at least one annual tabletop exercise validating the plan?
questions.supplementary_info: This fallback question provides partial credit for providers that cannot yet demonstrate a fully operational disconnect/reconnect capability but have documented procedures and evidence of tabletop testing.

questions.title: Reconnect
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST be able to reestablish all non-{{BLOC}}-connections after a disconnect in accordance of criterion SOV-4-9-C ("Disconnect") has been performed and has a process to install updates if the cloud environment was disconnected for a maximum of 90 days. The process to install updates if the cloud environment was disconnected for at most 90 days MUST also be tested.

questions.title: Open-Source Component Remote Tampering Controls
questions.text: Where the cloud service provider uses open-source software components, does it verify, document, and test that no open-source component includes remote features or mechanisms that could be used to materially tamper with or disrupt the cloud service without the provider's authorisation?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Annex II L2(j) requires specific controls for OSS remote-feature risks. Evidence: testing procedures for OSS remote features, change management covering firmware/BIOS/software updates, documented monitoring for OSS acquisition by 3rd-country entities.

questions.title: Infrastructure & Assets Located in EU
questions.text: Does the cloud computing service provider ensure that all infrastructure and assets used in the provision of the service — including those of subcontractors directly involved in delivery — are physically located within the Union, with no routing of operations through non-EU data centres or infrastructure for any part of the audited service?
questions.supplementary_info: Evidence per CADA Annex III §2: data centre location lists with precise addresses, network diagrams showing EU-exclusive architecture, asset registers, colocation agreements confirming EU location. At UAL 1, the location requirement applies unless the public sector body explicitly requires otherwise. At UAL 2, it is absolute.

questions.title: Workload Migration Ease — Reversibility
questions.text: Can workloads be migrated to, or integrated with, alternative {{BLOC}}-controlled solutions without vendor lock-in — demonstrated by a documented and tested workload migration path, export of data and configurations in open, documented formats, and the absence of contractual or technical egress barriers?
questions.supplementary_info: EU-CSF §4 SOV-4 contributing factor 1: "Ease of migrating workloads or integrating with alternative EU-controlled solutions without vendor lock-in." Evidence: documented migration runbook with tested execution, data export in open formats (e.g. OVF, CSV, SQL dump), configuration portability, and review of contract terms for absence of egress/termination lock-in clauses. Points and seal_contribution assigned as CSI editorial values; no point scale is defined in the source (DR-E27).
questions.eu_csf_source_factor: EU-CSF v1.2.1 §4, SOV-4 contributing factor 1: Ease of migrating workloads or integrating with alternative EU-controlled solutions without vendor lock-in.

questions.title: Migration Plan for Vendor Failure or Third-Country Restrictions
questions.text: For security-relevant software components provided, owned, or licensed by a legal entity established in a third country, does the provider maintain a documented migration plan covering vendor failure or third-country-imposed restrictions — including identified alternative solutions (open-source where possible) or a minimal-viable-functionality fallback, with implemented tests and a tested switchover plan?
questions.supplementary_info: Evidence per Annex III §9(3)(b)-(c): identified alternative solutions including open-source equivalents, a documented minimal-viable-functionality fallback where no equivalent exists, and an implemented and tested switchover plan. This question cross-tags the CSI reversibility set.
questions.cada_annex_ref: Annex II L2(i)(ii), L3(i)(ii); evidence per Annex III §9(3)(b)-(c)

questions.title: OSS Third-Country Acquisition Detection and Notice
questions.text: Where open-source software is used in the provision of the service, does the provider operate mechanisms to detect, and provide timely notice to the customer, if that software is acquired by or comes under the control of a third country or a legal entity or foundation established in a third country?
questions.cada_fidelity_rationale: The detection-and-notice mechanism appears in the Annex III audit-evidence catalogue for the open-source criterion, not in the L2(j) criterion text itself; tagged inferred accordingly.

questions.title: Support Personnel Residency — UAL 3 Increment
questions.text: Is all support for the cloud service (including support initiated and performed within the Union under L2(h)) delivered by personnel who are Union residents, and by third parties that are not subject to the control of a third country?
questions.supplementary_info: L2(h) requires support to be initiated and performed exclusively within the Union. L3(h) adds the personnel-residency and third-party-independence requirements. Both conditions must be met at UAL 3. Evidence: HR records or contractual confirmation of Union residency for all support staff, and supply-chain review confirming no third-country-controlled third parties deliver support.

questions.title: Business-Impact Analysis for Connectivity Loss (LMIC)
questions.text: For each critical workload, has a documented business-impact analysis defined the maximum tolerable period of disruption under loss of international connectivity or loss of the provider, and does the architecture demonstrably meet it (e.g., local caching, offline/degraded operating mode, in-country failover)?

source.clause: clause 8.2 (business-impact analysis and continuity requirements, cite by number only)

lmic_anchors.ref: business-impact analysis and continuity requirements (cite clause 8.2 by number only)
CP-2 (contingency plan); CP-10 (system recovery and reconstitution)
SOV-6-02 / SOV-6-02-AC (C3A vendor-loss continuity pattern)

questions.lmic_rationale: BIA-driven tolerable-disruption targets are the textbook ISO 22301 mechanism and public-domain NIST CP controls; C3A already applies the same logic to vendor loss (SOV-6-02). The question changes only the disruption scenario to the one LMICs face most concretely: subsea-cable or international-connectivity loss.

objectives.title: Supply Chain Sovereignty
objectives.description: Supply chain sovereignty evaluates the extent to which the cloud service provider can identify, manage, and reduce dependencies on non-EU software, hardware, and external services, and maintain operational continuity in the event of supply chain disruptions.

questions.title: Software Dependencies
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST identify, for each cloud service, the software components used and their respective countries of origin. A list of the relevant software suppliers and their country or countries for each service, MUST be compiled and available on demand to cloud service customers. The identification of the software components should be based on a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) (e.g. TR-03183-2) or achieve a comparable level of quality.

questions.title: Software Dependencies — Risk-Based Mitigation Process
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST maintain a risk-based process for identifying and mitigating dependencies on external software suppliers relevant to the operation of the cloud service. Where critical dependencies are identified, the cloud service provider MUST implement appropriate mitigation strategies and maintain architectural flexibility that enables substitution of software components. If it is not technically and reasonably feasible, this information MUST be adequately provided to the cloud service customer.
questions.supplementary_info: The terms software components and software suppliers refer exclusively to software used by the cloud service provider to deliver the cloud service. Software deployed by customers or marketplace providers is excluded. Software components under widely used open-source licenses may be excluded from origin reporting where license terms restrict redistribution of such information.

questions.title: Hardware Dependencies
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST maintain a documented inventory of the hardware components used to provide cloud services. A list of the relevant hardware suppliers and their country or countries MUST be compiled and available on demand to cloud service customers.

questions.title: Hardware Dependencies — Risk-Based Mitigation Process
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST maintain a risk-based process for identifying and mitigating dependencies on hardware suppliers relevant to the operation of the cloud service. Where critical dependencies are identified, the cloud service provider MUST implement mitigation strategies and maintain architectural flexibility enabling substitution of hardware components. If it is not technically and operationally feasible, this information MUST be adequately provided to the cloud service customer.

questions.title: External Service Dependencies
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST maintain a documented inventory of used external cloud services that are necessary for the delivery of the cloud service. The list of information regarding the relevant external service providers and the country or countries of service provision or development MUST be made available to cloud service customers.

questions.title: External Service Dependencies — Documented Management Process
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST maintain a documented process for identifying and managing external service dependencies relevant to the delivery of the cloud service. Where critical dependencies are identified, the cloud service provider MUST implement mitigation strategies and maintain architectural flexibility enabling substitution of service dependencies. If it is not technically and operationally feasible, this information MUST be adequately provided to the cloud service customer.
questions.supplementary_info: External services refer exclusively to services that are functionally required for the provision of the cloud service itself. External cloud services refer to services provided by third-party cloud providers that are integrated into the delivery of the primary cloud service but are operated and maintained by separate providers.

questions.title: Export Restriction
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST maintain documented processes for identifying and mitigating risks related to export restrictions or supply chain disruptions affecting software, external services, and hardware used in the delivery of the cloud service. Where such restrictions may materially affect the operation of the cloud service, the cloud service provider MUST inform affected customers.
questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-2 CF: 'Applicability of international regimes, which may restrict usage or transfer.' Export restrictions are supply-chain continuity risks classified under SOV-5 following C3A §2.5.4, where they appear as supply chain risks rather than legal/jurisdictional risks.

questions.title: Capacity Management
questions.supplementary_info: C5:2026 (Cloud Computing Compliance Criteria Catalogue) is a cloud security standard published by the German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI). OPS-01/02/03 cover capacity management: planning for future demand (OPS-01), monitoring current usage (OPS-02), and controlling resource allocation (OPS-03). See: https://www.bsi.bund.de/EN/Themen/Unternehmen-und-Organisationen/Informationen-und-Empfehlungen/Empfehlungen-nach-Angriffszielen/Cloud-Computing/Kriterienkatalog-C5/kriterienkatalog-c5_node.html

bloc.text: Capacity management covering resource planning, usage monitoring, and capacity control MUST be performed within {{BLOC}}.
bloc.text_generalized: Capacity management covering resource planning, usage monitoring, and capacity control MUST be performed within {{BLOC}}.
bloc.text_c3a: Capacity management MUST be performed in {{BLOC}} in accordance with C5:2026 (OPS-01 Capacity Management – Planning, OPS-02 – Monitoring, OPS-03 – Controlling of Resources).

national.text: Capacity management covering resource planning, usage monitoring, and capacity control MUST be performed within {{COUNTRY}}.
national.text_generalized: Capacity management covering resource planning, usage monitoring, and capacity control MUST be performed within {{COUNTRY}}.
national.text_c3a: Capacity management MUST be performed in {{COUNTRY}} in accordance with C5:2026 (OPS-01 Capacity Management – Planning, OPS-02 – Monitoring, OPS-03 – Controlling of Resources).

questions.eu_csf_fidelity_rationale: Derived from EU-CSF SOV-4 CF: 'Capacity for EU operators to manage, maintain, and support the technology.' Capacity management performed within the EU ensures that scaling decisions and resource allocation remain under EU-controlled operations. Placed under SOV-5 following C3A §2.5.5, which classifies it as a supply-chain capacity control.

questions.title: Subcontractor Transparency & Sovereignty Due Diligence
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider maintain a complete, up-to-date register of all subcontractors involved in service delivery — including their country of establishment, access rights, and role — and conduct structured sovereignty due diligence on each subcontractor covering extraterritorial legal exposure, control chains, and EU data access restrictions?
questions.supplementary_info: CADA Annex II L1(f) requires full subcontractor transparency and due diligence as a baseline UAL 1 criterion. Evidence: published or contractually disclosed subcontractor list, due diligence questionnaire results, contractual flow-down of sovereignty obligations.

questions.title: Prohibition on Third-Country Control over Software Components
questions.text: For software components or products used in the provision of the service, does the provider demonstrate that no third country or legal entity established in a third country holds or exercises effective control over their design, development, maintenance, and evolution — including the ability to materially influence technical evolution, maintenance priorities, security remediation, or long-term continuity of the component?

questions.title: Support Substitutability Ladder (LMIC)
questions.text: If the current support vendor for the platform software fails, exits the market, changes licensing terms, or becomes subject to third-country restrictions, what is the demonstrated path to continued supported operation?

ladder.label: Same deployed stack is supportable by at least one alternative vendor or in-house team under an existing written arrangement (takeover support contract, escrowed support agreement, or documented in-house capability), without redeployment.

Support switch requires control-plane redeployment on an alternative distribution, but workloads and data port via standards-based APIs and formats, AND a control-plane rebuild runbook exists and has been exercised.
Exit from the current platform requires re-platforming workloads (no portable path), or no documented rebuild path exists.

lmic_anchors.ref: Art. 29 (assessment of substitutability of ICT third-party providers; concentration risk)
§12.2 (due diligence incl. substitutability), §15 (exit strategies)
OpenStack Powered guidelines (board-approved guideline 2020.11 + add-on programs), must-pass capability tests; conformance demonstrated via RefStack results
SOV-6-02/-02-AC (C3A vendor-loss continuity)

questions.lmic_rationale: Substitutability of critical providers is a named supervisory requirement (DORA Art. 29; EBA §12.2), not an invention. The three tiers encode the empirical structure of the market: identical-stack takeover support is rare even for open platforms; what open standards genuinely deliver is workload/data portability across distributions (anchored by the OpenInfra interoperability certification — guideline 2020.11, validated via RefStack), which is Tier B; proprietary platforms whose exit means re-platforming workloads are Tier C. The Tier B gate (exercised rebuild runbook) prevents paper reversibility: a portable data plane without a tested control-plane rebuild path is Tier C in any real crisis. DR-L7 records the OpenStack Powered guideline vintage 2020.11.

questions.title: Alternative Support Arrangement (EV1)
questions.text: Provide the written alternative-support arrangement for the currently deployed stack (takeover support contract, escrowed support agreement, or documented in-house capability with named, trained personnel).

lmic_anchors.ref: §15 (exit strategy documentation)
SA-9, SR-6 (external services and supplier review — public domain, quotable)

questions.lmic_rationale: Tier A is only credible against a written arrangement; a sales-deck assurance is a vendor claim and cannot satisfy a gate (locked design decision 4).

questions.title: Cross-Distribution API Portability Evidence (EV2)
questions.text: Demonstrate workload and data-plane portability across distributions: standards-based API conformance (e.g., interoperability certification or documented conformance test results), standard image/volume formats, and tenant configuration export.

source.clause: OpenStack Powered guidelines (board-approved guideline 2020.11 + add-on programs), must-pass capability tests; conformance demonstrated via RefStack results

lmic_anchors.ref: OpenStack Powered guidelines (board-approved guideline 2020.11 + add-on programs), must-pass capability tests; conformance demonstrated via RefStack results
portability concepts (cite clause only)
Art. 30 (technical aspects of switching — the pattern for standards-based portability evidence)

questions.lmic_rationale: Distinguishes genuine API-level portability from marketing claims of openness; conformance certification (OpenStack Powered guideline 2020.11 via RefStack) is the citable instrument for exactly this. DR-L7 records the guideline vintage.

questions.title: Exercised Control-Plane Rebuild Runbook (EV3)
questions.text: Provide the control-plane rebuild runbook for redeployment on an alternative distribution, and evidence it has been exercised (test report of a representative rebuild — full or scoped — with date, scope, duration, and outcome).

lmic_anchors.ref: §15 (exit plans incl. testing considerations)
Art. 28(8) (exit strategies for critical services)
SOV-6-12-LMIC / SOV-4-15-CADA tested-switchover pattern (CADA Annex III §9(3)(b)-(c))

questions.lmic_rationale: MAINTAINER-LOCKED GATE: an unexercised runbook is paper reversibility. Same tested-switchover standard CADA’s audit evidence demands and EBA/DORA expect for critical exits; only the application point (control plane) is specific.

questions.title: Customization Drift and OSS Currency Control (LMIC)
questions.text: Where the platform is open-source or source-available, does the operator maintain: (a) a documented register of deviations from the upstream project or supported distribution baseline; (b) a defined upgrade cadence with evidence of staying within N-2 of supported releases; and (c) confirmation that deviations do not break standards-based API conformance relied on for portability?

lmic_anchors.ref: CM-2 (baseline configuration), CM-3 (configuration change control) — public domain, quotable
SOV-5-01-AC (C3A risk-based dependency process); SOV-4-16-CADA family (OSS currency/maintenance risks, CADA Annex III §10(2))
Annex III §10(2)(b) (up-to-date OSS applied without undue delay)

questions.lmic_rationale: A heavily forked ‘open’ platform that only its builders understand has the reversibility profile of proprietary software with none of the support. Baseline/change control is verbatim NIST CM territory and OSS-currency risk is in CADA’s own audit evidence; the question composes the two. Failing clause (c) degrades the SOV-5-08 ladder answer: deviations breaking API conformance invalidate the Tier B portability premise.

objectives.title: Technology Sovereignty
objectives.description: Technology sovereignty assesses whether the cloud service provider maintains independent access to source code, software development capability, and contingency strategies to sustain service delivery without dependence on third parties.

questions.title: Source Code Availability
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST have a backup of the source code in {{BLOC}} that is not older than 24 hours and contains at minimum 5 versions of the cloud services so that the operation of the cloud service is possible at any time without external dependencies. This includes all infrastructure-as-code build-scripts and deployment toolchains. The local source code backup MUST include a documentation that enables the cloud service provider to independently work with the source code and develop it further at any time without external dependencies.

questions.title: Source Code — Local Build Capability
questions.text: Does the cloud service provider maintain a local build environment in {{COUNTRY}} capable of compiling, testing, and deploying the cloud service from source code, with the engineering talent to operate it autonomously for at least 90 days without external dependencies, even if the source code itself is mirrored from a non-{{COUNTRY}} master?
questions.supplementary_info: Fallback for SOV-6-01 where holding a local source code backup is infeasible (e.g. resold hyperscaler service). This rewards providers that have invested in the operational capability to build and deploy the service from source — even if the source itself originates abroad. The 90-day window aligns with C3A SOV-4-10 (reconnect window).

questions.title: Source Code — Documented Exit & Tested Data Export
questions.text: Where the cloud service provider cannot guarantee local source code availability, does it maintain a documented exit and portability plan with at least one tested customer data export within the past 12 months, demonstrating that customer data, derived data, and account data can be returned to the customer in a standard portable format within a contractually-defined timeline?
questions.supplementary_info: Customer-side reversibility fallback for SOV-6-01. Consistent with the reversibility dimension of the CSI methodology. Where the provider cannot demonstrate operational independence, tested portability ensures the customer is not stranded.

questions.title: Continuous Service Delivery
questions.text: In the event of disconnection of third parties, the cloud service provider MUST maintain documented contingency strategies ensuring continued secure delivery of the cloud services. These strategies may include alternative software suppliers, internal remediation capabilities, or compensating security controls.

questions.title: Continuous Service Delivery — Engineering Capability
questions.text: In the event of disruption or loss of an external software vendor, the cloud service provider MUST maintain the capability to remediate software vulnerabilities and implement necessary changes. The cloud provider MUST maintain specialized engineering talent and local build-environments necessary to compile, test, and deploy emergency security patches to the cloud services independently of third parties.

questions.title: Software Development
questions.text: The cloud service provider MUST ensure that authorised personnel have access to the software development tools and environments necessary to maintain and update the cloud services. The cloud service provider MUST also maintain documented contingency procedures for scenarios in which access to critical software development tools or development environment dependencies is disrupted, ensuring the continued ability to maintain and update the cloud services.

questions.title: EU HPC Independence
questions.text: For compute-intensive workloads (AI/ML inference, data analytics, simulation), can the cloud service provider demonstrate that its high-performance computing capability — including the processors, accelerators (GPUs/TPUs), and software ecosystems used — is sourced from EU-controlled or EU-accessible supply chains, with no critical dependency on a single non-EU semiconductor vendor whose withdrawal would materially impair the service?
